topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The spreadsheet glowed on my monitor, a stark contrast to the knot tightening in my stomach. I had meticulously cross-referenced the quarterly reports, a task that usually brought a quiet sense of accomplishment. Then I saw the numbers for Alex. A sudden, sharp intake of breath escaped me. His compensation package, when compared to mine, was staggering. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, the keys feeling suddenly heavy and unresponsive. I forced myself to look away, focusing instead on the dust motes dancing in the afternoon sunlight slanting across the cubicle wall. Every calculated figure seemed to mock my own meager salary. I pushed my chair back slightly, the scrape of the wheels against the linoleum sounding deafening in the silence of my workspace.

[story 2]

"Just checking the payroll summary," I mumbled to myself, my voice sounding unnaturally high. The screen displayed the figures, and the reality hit me like a physical blow. Sarah, the new intern who had been around for barely six months, was earning significantly more than I was. A coldness seeped into my limbs, making my hands clammy. I gripped the edge of my desk until my knuckles were white. I tried to rationalize it—maybe her role was more senior, or perhaps I was simply misinterpreting the data. But the numbers were undeniable. A slow, heavy dread settled in my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I stared blankly at the beige partition wall, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The office hummed with the usual low thrum of activity, but for me, everything felt muted, distant. I was reviewing the departmental budget, a routine activity that had become an ordeal. When I finally landed on the comparative salary data, a wave of nausea washed over me. Liam, a junior associate, was earning nearly double what I was making. My jaw tightened involuntarily. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. I picked up my coffee mug, but the warmth did nothing to soothe the prickling sensation on my skin. I took a long, slow sip, the bitter liquid doing little to settle the churning feeling beneath my ribs. I needed to leave. Now.
